description: This figure presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of impact fracture surfaces for Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel under three heat treatment conditions, with low-magnification reduction maps inset in the lower-left corner of each panel. Subfigure (a) shows sample S1000 (single-stage heat treatment at 1000 °C for 3 min), exhibiting quasi-cleavage fracture features characterized by flat facets, together with microcracks and microvoids (scale bar 5 μm). Subfigure (b) displays sample S1050 (single-stage heat treatment at 1050 °C for 3 min), showing similar quasi-cleavage morphology but with apparent differences in microvoid distribution and crack propagation paths (scale bar 10 μm). Subfigure (c) reveals sample MHT (multi-stage heat treatment: 1000 °C/3 min + 750 °C/15 min + 1050 °C/3 min), which exhibits a dramatic transition to ductile dimple fracture morphology, indicating significantly enhanced toughness. The progression from quasi-cleavage in S1000/S1050 to dimpled rupture in MHT visually confirms the caption's report that optimizing grain boundaries via multi-stage heat treatment increases impact toughness by 88.6% without compromising strength, as the fracture mechanism shifts from brittle transgranular cracking to energy-absorbing microvoid coalescence.
